subject: Physics
q: In Young's double slit interference experiment, the slit widths are in the ratio $1: 25$. Then the ratio of intensity at the maxima and minima in the interference pattern is
question: In Young's double slit interference experiment, the slit widths are in the ratio $1: 25$. Then the ratio of intensity at the maxima and minima in the interference pattern is
solution:
We know that,
$$
\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{\left(\sqrt{\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2}+1}\right)^2}{\left(\sqrt{\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2}-1}\right)^2}
$$
$I_{\max }$ and $I_{\min }$ are maximum and minimum intensity
$\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ are widths of two slits

$$
\therefore \frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{\left(\sqrt{\frac{1}{25}}+1\right)^2}{\left(\sqrt{\frac{1}{25}}-1\right)^2}\left(\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2}=\frac{1}{25} \text { given }\right)
$$
On solving we get,
$$
\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{\frac{36}{25}}{\frac{16}{25}}=\frac{9}{4}=9: 4
$$

subject: Physics
q: An unpolarized light of intensity $I_o$ passes through polarizer and then through a certain optically active solution and finally it goes to analyser. If the angle between analyser and polariser is $0°$ and intensity of light emerged from analyser is $\dfrac{3}{8}I_o$, the angle of rotation of the light by the solution with respect to analyser is _______ degrees.
question: An unpolarized light of intensity $I_o$ passes through polarizer and then through a certain optically active solution and finally it goes to analyser. If the angle between analyser and polariser is $0°$ and intensity of light emerged from analyser is $\dfrac{3}{8}I_o$, the angle of rotation of the light by the solution with respect to analyser is _______ degrees.
solution:
Intensity of light after passing through the first polarizer is:

$I_1 = \dfrac{I_o}{2}$

Let the optically active solution rotate the plane of polarization by an angle $\theta$. Since the angle between the polarizer and the analyser is $0^\circ$, the angle between the plane of polarization of the light emerging from the solution and the transmission axis of the analyser is also $\theta$.

According to Malus's law, the intensity of light emerging from the analyser is:

$I_2 = I_1 \cos^2 \theta = \dfrac{I_o}{2} \cos^2 \theta$

Given that the final intensity is $\dfrac{3}{8}I_o$, we have:

$\dfrac{I_o}{2} \cos^2 \theta = \dfrac{3}{8}I_o$

$\cos^2 \theta = \dfrac{3}{4}$

$\cos \theta = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$

$\theta = 30^\circ$

Answer: $30$

subject: Physics
q: The Young's double slit interference experiment is performed using light consisting of 480 nm and 600 nm wavelengths to form interference patterns. The least number of the bright fringes of 480 nm light that are required for the first coincidence with the bright fringes formed by 600 nm light is
question: The Young's double slit interference experiment is performed using light consisting of 480 nm and 600 nm wavelengths to form interference patterns. The least number of the bright fringes of 480 nm light that are required for the first coincidence with the bright fringes formed by 600 nm light is
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{n_1 \lambda_1 D}{d}=\frac{n_2 \lambda_2 D}{d} \\
& n 480=m 600 \\
& n_{\min }=5
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The two light beams having intensities <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mi>I</mi></math> interfere to produce a fringe pattern on a screen. The phase difference between the beams is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> at point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi></math> at point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>. Then the difference between the resultant intensities at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> will be :
question: The two light beams having intensities <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mi>I</mi></math> interfere to produce a fringe pattern on a screen. The phase difference between the beams is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> at point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi></math> at point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>. Then the difference between the resultant intensities at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> will be :
solution:
Resultant intensity is given by, Iresultant=I1+I2+2I1I2cosϕ

At P, IP=9I+I+29I×Icosπ2=10I

At Q, IQ=9I+I+29I×Icosπ=10I-2×3I=4I

Now, difference between intensities is 10I-4I=6I

subject: Physics
q: Two coherent monochromatic light beams of intensities 4I and 9I are superimposed. The difference between the maximum and minimum intensities in the resulting interference pattern is xI. The value of $x$ is ______.
question: Two coherent monochromatic light beams of intensities 4I and 9I are superimposed. The difference between the maximum and minimum intensities in the resulting interference pattern is xI. The value of $x$ is ______.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}_{\max }=\left(\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_1}+\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_2}\right)^2 \\ & =(\sqrt{4 \mathrm{I}}+\sqrt{9 \mathrm{I}})^2=25 \mathrm{I} \\ & \mathrm{I}_{\min }=\left(\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_1}-\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_2}\right)^2 \\ & =(\sqrt{4 \mathrm{I}}-\sqrt{9 \mathrm{I}})^2=\mathrm{I} \\ & \mathrm{I}_{\max }-\mathrm{I}_{\min }=24 \mathrm{I} \\ & \mathrm{x}=24\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The width of fringe is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> on the screen in a double slit experiment for the light of wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math>. The width of the fringe for the light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> will be:
question: The width of fringe is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> on the screen in a double slit experiment for the light of wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math>. The width of the fringe for the light of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> will be:
solution:
For a light of wavelength λ, the fringe width in double slit experiment is given by

β=λDd   ...(1)

For a light of wavelength λ', the fringe width is given by

β'=λ'Dd   ...2

Divide equation (2) by equation (1) and simplify to obtain the fringe width for the later case.

β'β=λ'DdλDd= λ'λ⇒β'= λ'λβ   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required fringe width.

β'=600 nm400 nm×2 mm= 3 mm

q: Two polaroids have their polarizing directions parallel so that the intensity of a transmitted light is maximum. The angle through which either polaroid must be turned if the intensity is to drop by one-half is
question: Two polaroids have their polarizing directions parallel so that the intensity of a transmitted light is maximum. The angle through which either polaroid must be turned if the intensity is to drop by one-half is
solution:
For $I=\frac{I_0}{2}$ and $I=I_0 \cos ^2 \theta=\frac{I_0}{2}$ $\therefore \theta=45^{\circ}$
Therefore the angle through which either polaroids turned is $135^{\circ}\left(=180^{\circ}-45^{\circ}\right)$

subject: Physics
q: A parallel beam of monochromatic light of wavelength $600 \mathrm{~nm}$ passes through single slit of $0.4 \mathrm{~mm}$ width. Angular divergence corresponding to second order minima would be ______ $\times 10^{-3} \mathrm{rad}$.
question: A parallel beam of monochromatic light of wavelength $600 \mathrm{~nm}$ passes through single slit of $0.4 \mathrm{~mm}$ width. Angular divergence corresponding to second order minima would be ______ $\times 10^{-3} \mathrm{rad}$.
solution:
$\sin \theta \simeq \theta \simeq \frac{2 \lambda}{b}$
$=\frac{2 \times 600 \times 10^{-9}}{4 \times 10^{-4}}=3 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{rad}$
Total divergence $=(3+3) \times 10^{-3}=6 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{rad}$

q: The angular width of the central maximum in a single slit diffraction pattern is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> . The width of the slit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μm</mi></math>. The slit is illuminated by monochromatic plane waves. If another slit of the same width is made near it, Young's fringes can be observed on a screen placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>cm</mi></math> from the slits. If the observed fringe width is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>cm</mi></math>, what is slit separation distance? (i.e., the distance between the centres of each slit.)
question: The angular width of the central maximum in a single slit diffraction pattern is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> . The width of the slit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μm</mi></math>. The slit is illuminated by monochromatic plane waves. If another slit of the same width is made near it, Young's fringes can be observed on a screen placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>cm</mi></math> from the slits. If the observed fringe width is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>cm</mi></math>, what is slit separation distance? (i.e., the distance between the centres of each slit.)
solution:
In diffraction, dsin30°=λ, λ=d2



Young’s fringe width [ d'- the separation between two slits] 

β=λ×Dd'⇒10-2=d2×50×10-2d'
⇒10-2=10-6×50×10-22×d'⇒d'=25 μm.

q: In a Young's double slit experiment, a laser light of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>560</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> produces an interference pattern with consecutive bright fringes' separation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. Now another light is used to produce an interference pattern with consecutive bright fringes' separation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The wavelength of second light is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>nm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: In a Young's double slit experiment, a laser light of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>560</mn><mo> </mo><mi>nm</mi></math> produces an interference pattern with consecutive bright fringes' separation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. Now another light is used to produce an interference pattern with consecutive bright fringes' separation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The wavelength of second light is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>nm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Given here: λ1=560×10-9 m, β1=7.2×10-3 m and β2=8.1×10-3 m.

Let d be the width and D be the distance between slit and screen then fringe width, β=λDd.

Thus, from above,  β∝λ

So, the wavelength of second light is λ2=β2β1λ1=98λ1

∴   λ2=98×560=630 nm.

subject: Physics
q: If the measured angular separation between the second minimum to the left of the central maximum and the third minimum to the right of the central maximum is $30^{\circ}$ in a single slit diffraction pattern recorded using 628 nm light, then the width of the slit is _______ $\mu \mathrm{m}$.
question: If the measured angular separation between the second minimum to the left of the central maximum and the third minimum to the right of the central maximum is $30^{\circ}$ in a single slit diffraction pattern recorded using 628 nm light, then the width of the slit is _______ $\mu \mathrm{m}$.
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \theta_1=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \lambda}{a}\right) \\ & \theta_2=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{3 \lambda}{a}\right) \\ & \because \quad \theta_1+\theta_2=30^{\circ}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \lambda}{\mathrm{a}}\right)+\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{3 \lambda}{\mathrm{a}}\right)=\frac{\pi}{6} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2 \lambda}{\mathrm{a}} \sqrt{1-\left(\frac{3 \lambda}{\mathrm{a}}\right)^2}+\frac{3 \lambda}{\mathrm{a}} \sqrt{1+\left(\frac{2 \lambda}{\mathrm{a}}\right)^2}=\sin \frac{\pi}{6}\end{aligned}$
Here $\lambda=628 \mathrm{~nm}$
After solving
$\mathrm{A}=6.07 \mu \mathrm{~m}$
Approximate Method :
$\begin{aligned} & \theta=\theta_1+\theta_2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}+\frac{3 \lambda}{a} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{5}{a}(628 \mathrm{~nm}) \\ & \Rightarrow a=6 \mu \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: In Young's double slit experiment, carried out with light of wavelength $5000 \AA$, the distance between the slits is $0.3 \mathrm{~mm}$ and the screen is at $200 \mathrm{~cm}$ from the slits. The central maximum is at $x=0 \mathrm{~cm}$. The value of $x$ for third maxima is _____$\mathrm{mm}$.
question: In Young's double slit experiment, carried out with light of wavelength $5000 \AA$, the distance between the slits is $0.3 \mathrm{~mm}$ and the screen is at $200 \mathrm{~cm}$ from the slits. The central maximum is at $x=0 \mathrm{~cm}$. The value of $x$ for third maxima is _____$\mathrm{mm}$.
solution:
$\beta=\frac{\lambda \mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{5 \times 10^{-7} \times 2}{3 \times 10^{-4}}=\frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{3} \mathrm{~m}$
For $3^{\text {rd }}$ maxima $y_3=3 \beta=10 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}=10 \mathrm{~mm}$

subject: Physics
q: The maximum intensity in a Young's double slit experiment is $I_0$. Distance between the slits ($d$) is $5\lambda$, where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of light used. The intensity of the fringe, exactly opposite to one of the slits on the screen, placed at $D = 10d$ is _______.
question: The maximum intensity in a Young's double slit experiment is $I_0$. Distance between the slits ($d$) is $5\lambda$, where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of light used. The intensity of the fringe, exactly opposite to one of the slits on the screen, placed at $D = 10d$ is _______.
solution:
The position of the point on the screen exactly opposite to one of the slits is at a distance $y = \dfrac{d}{2}$ from the central maximum.

The path difference $\Delta x$ at this point is given by:
$\Delta x = \dfrac{yd}{D}$

Substituting $y = \dfrac{d}{2}$ and $D = 10d$:
$\Delta x = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)d}{10d} = \dfrac{d^2}{20d} = \dfrac{d}{20}$

Given that the distance between the slits is $d = 5\lambda$, we have:
$\Delta x = \dfrac{5\lambda}{20} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4}$

The corresponding phase difference $\Delta \phi$ is:
$\Delta \phi = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} \Delta x = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4}\right) = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$

The intensity at this point is given by:
$I = I_0 \cos^2\left(\dfrac{\Delta \phi}{2}\right)$

Substituting $\Delta \phi = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$:
$I = I_0 \cos^2\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right) = I_0 \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 = \dfrac{I_0}{2}$

Answer: $\dfrac{I_0}{2}$

subject: Physics
q: An unpolarised light is incident at an interface of two dielectric media having refractive indices of 2 (incident medium) and $2 \sqrt{3}$ (medium) respectively. To satisfy the condition that reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular to each other, the angle of incidence is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: An unpolarised light is incident at an interface of two dielectric media having refractive indices of 2 (incident medium) and $2 \sqrt{3}$ (medium) respectively. To satisfy the condition that reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular to each other, the angle of incidence is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
For reflected and refracted rays to be perpendicular, if $i$ is the angle of incidence and $r$ is the angle of refraction, then $i + r = 90°$, giving $r = 90° - i$.
Applying Snell's law: $n_1 \sin i = n_2 \sin r$
$2 \sin i = 2\sqrt{3} \sin(90° - i)$
$2 \sin i = 2\sqrt{3} \cos i$
$\tan i = \sqrt{3}$
$i = 60°$

subject: Physics
q: An unpolarized light of certain intensity passes through a combination of two polarizers whose transmission axes are at $30°$ and $90°$, respectively, with respect to the horizontal axis. A third polarizer with its transmission axis at $60°$ with the horizontal axis is placed between the two existing polarizers. The ratio of the output intensities with and without the third polarizer is ______.
question: An unpolarized light of certain intensity passes through a combination of two polarizers whose transmission axes are at $30°$ and $90°$, respectively, with respect to the horizontal axis. A third polarizer with its transmission axis at $60°$ with the horizontal axis is placed between the two existing polarizers. The ratio of the output intensities with and without the third polarizer is ______.
solution:
Let the intensity of the incident unpolarized light be $I_0$.

After passing through the first polarizer, the intensity becomes $I_1 = \dfrac{I_0}{2}$.

Without the third polarizer, the light passes directly from the first polarizer (at $30^{\circ}$) to the second polarizer (at $90^{\circ}$). The angle between their transmission axes is $90^{\circ} - 30^{\circ} = 60^{\circ}$.

Using Malus's law, the output intensity without the third polarizer is:
$I_{without} = I_1 \cos^2(60^{\circ}) = \dfrac{I_0}{2} \times \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{I_0}{8}$

When the third polarizer (at $60^{\circ}$) is placed between them, the light first passes from the first polarizer to the third polarizer. The angle between their axes is $60^{\circ} - 30^{\circ} = 30^{\circ}$.

The intensity after the third polarizer is:
$I_3 = I_1 \cos^2(30^{\circ}) = \dfrac{I_0}{2} \times \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{3I_0}{8}$

The light then passes from the third polarizer to the second polarizer. The angle between their axes is $90^{\circ} - 60^{\circ} = 30^{\circ}$.

The final output intensity with the third polarizer is:
$I_{with} = I_3 \cos^2(30^{\circ}) = \dfrac{3I_0}{8} \times \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{9I_0}{32}$

The ratio of the output intensities with and without the third polarizer is:
$\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{I_{with}}{I_{without}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{9I_0}{32}}{\dfrac{I_0}{8}} = \dfrac{9}{4}$

Answer: $9/4$

Statement-2 : Radio waves have greater frequency compared to microwaves.
question:
This question has Statement-1 and Statements2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement-1 : Out of radio waves and microwaves, the radio waves undergo more diffraction.
Statement-2 : Radio waves have greater frequency compared to microwaves.
options: Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true and Statement-2 is the correct explanation of Statement-1
Statement-1 is false, Statement-2 is true.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is false.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true but Statement-2 is not the correct explanation of Statement-1
solution:
Wavelength of radio waves is greater than microwaves hence frequency of radio waves is less than microwaves.
The degree of diffraction is greater whose wavelength is greater.

subject: Physics
q: A double slit interference experiment performed with a light of wavelength 600 nm forms an interference fringe pattern on a screen with 10 th bright fringe having its centre at a distance of 10 mm from the central maximum. Distance of the centre of the same 10th bright fringe from the central maximum when the source of light is replaced by another source of wavelength 660 nm would be _________ mm .
question: A double slit interference experiment performed with a light of wavelength 600 nm forms an interference fringe pattern on a screen with 10 th bright fringe having its centre at a distance of 10 mm from the central maximum. Distance of the centre of the same 10th bright fringe from the central maximum when the source of light is replaced by another source of wavelength 660 nm would be _________ mm .
solution:
Position of the $n^{\text {th }}$ bright fringe w.r.t. central maxima
in a YDSE is $y_n=n \frac{\lambda D}{d}$.
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{y_{10}^{\prime}}{y_{10}}=\frac{\lambda^{\prime}}{\lambda}$
or $\quad y_{10}^{\prime}=\frac{\lambda^{\prime}}{\lambda} y_{10}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\left(\frac{660 \mathrm{~nm}}{600 \mathrm{~nm}}\right) 10 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & =11 \mathrm{~mm}\end{aligned}$

q: In young's double slit experiment, the fringe width is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. If the entire arrangement is placed in water of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math>, then the fringe width becomes (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mm</mi></math>)
question: In young's double slit experiment, the fringe width is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. If the entire arrangement is placed in water of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math>, then the fringe width becomes (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mm</mi></math>)
solution:
For a given light wavelength corresponding a medium of refractive index μ

λmed =λvacumm μ.

And we know that fringe width β=λD d

Therefore, βmed =βvacuum μ=1243=9 mm

subject: Physics
q: Two beams of light having intensities <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>I</mi></math> interfere to produce a fringe pattern on a screen. The phase difference between the two beams are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> at points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> respectively. The difference between the resultant intensities at the two points is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mi>I</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be _____ .
question: Two beams of light having intensities <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>I</mi></math> interfere to produce a fringe pattern on a screen. The phase difference between the two beams are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> at points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> respectively. The difference between the resultant intensities at the two points is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mi>I</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be _____ .
solution:
Given here, phase difference at point A and B are ϕA=π2and ϕB=π3.

Resultant intensity at point A, 

IA=I+4I+2I4Icosπ2

⇒IA=5I+4I0=5I

Resultant intensity at point B,

IB=I+4I+2I4Icos60° =5I+4I×12=7I

Difference in intensities is IB-IA=7I-5I=2I.

Thus, value of x=2.

q: In a Young’s double slit experiment, the separation between the slits is <math><mn>0.15</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> . In the experiment, a source of light of wavelength <math><mn>589</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is used and the interference pattern is observed on a screen kept <math><mn>1.5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> away. The separation between the successive bright fringes on the screen is:
question: In a Young’s double slit experiment, the separation between the slits is <math><mn>0.15</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> . In the experiment, a source of light of wavelength <math><mn>589</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is used and the interference pattern is observed on a screen kept <math><mn>1.5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> away. The separation between the successive bright fringes on the screen is:
solution: β=λDd=589×10-9×1.50.15×10-3=5.9mm

subject: Physics
q: A slit of width $a$ is illuminated by light of wavelength $\lambda$. The linear separation between $1^{st}$ and $3^{rd}$ minima in the diffraction pattern produced on a screen placed at a distance $D$ from the slit system is _____.
question: A slit of width $a$ is illuminated by light of wavelength $\lambda$. The linear separation between $1^{st}$ and $3^{rd}$ minima in the diffraction pattern produced on a screen placed at a distance $D$ from the slit system is _____.
solution:
The position of the $n^{th}$ minimum in a single slit diffraction pattern on a screen at distance $D$ is given by $y_n = \dfrac{n D \lambda}{a}$.

For the $1^{st}$ minimum, $n = 1$, so $y_1 = \dfrac{D \lambda}{a}$.

For the $3^{rd}$ minimum, $n = 3$, so $y_3 = \dfrac{3 D \lambda}{a}$.

The linear separation between the $1^{st}$ and $3^{rd}$ minima on the same side of the central maximum is $\Delta y = y_3 - y_1$.

$\Delta y = \dfrac{3 D \lambda}{a} - \dfrac{D \lambda}{a} = 2\dfrac{D \lambda}{a}$.

Answer: $2\dfrac{D\lambda}{a}$

subject: Physics
q: The maximum number of possible interference maxima for slit separation equal to $1.8 \lambda$, where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of light used, in a Young's double slit experiment is
question: The maximum number of possible interference maxima for slit separation equal to $1.8 \lambda$, where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of light used, in a Young's double slit experiment is
solution:
As $\sin \theta=\frac{n \lambda}{d}$ and $\sin \theta$ cannot be $\ngtr 1$
$$
\therefore 1=\frac{n \lambda}{1.8 \lambda}
$$
or $n=1.8$
Hence maximum number of possible interference maximas, $0, \pm 1$ i.e. 3

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following phenomena can not be explained by wave theory of light?
question: Which of the following phenomena can not be explained by wave theory of light?
solution: Compton effect refers to scattering of a photon by free electrons. This phenomenon provides an evidence for particle nature of light.

q: The light waves from two coherent sources have same intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> In interference pattern the intensity of light at minima is zero. What will be the intensity of light at maxima?
question: The light waves from two coherent sources have same intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> In interference pattern the intensity of light at minima is zero. What will be the intensity of light at maxima?
solution:
Iminimum =0

I1-I22=0

⇒  I1=I2

Imax=I1+I22=4I0
